hypotheses.proxy_description: Count and total annual processing capacity (tonnes/year) of full-scale food waste and yard waste composting facilities
hypotheses.mechanism: Composting capacity sets ceiling on possible recovery rates—no country can recover more waste than its infrastructure allows. Between 2013 and 2023, approximately 40 new facilities began operations, coinciding with expansion of state organics waste bans and municipal composting mandates.

data_source.name: BioCycle Nationwide Survey on Full-Scale Food Waste Composting Infrastructure
data_source.coverage: 105+ facilities across 44 US states, annual updates

hypotheses.literature_evidence: 40 new facilities began operations between 2013-2023, coinciding with state organics waste bans and municipal composting mandates (BioCycle 2023)
caveats: Facility capacity does not equal actual processing; utilization rates vary 30-90%
Lag structure of 1-3 years between construction and operation

context.geographic_scope: Global; most complete for OECD countries and China

hypotheses.proxy_description: Number of operational waste-to-combustion facilities with energy recovery capability, measured as count and megawatts of installed electricity generation capacity
hypotheses.mechanism: Each WtE facility that operates diverts mass from landfill. In Denmark, Switzerland, and Belgium, incineration with energy recovery accounts for 50-70% of waste treatment, mechanistically driving elevated WRR. EPA reports 75 operational facilities process ~14% of municipal solid waste in the US.
hypotheses.literature_evidence: 75 operational waste combustion facilities process ~14% of municipal solid waste (EPA 2023); incineration accounts for 50-70% of waste treatment in Denmark, Switzerland, Belgium
caveats: Installed capacity ≠ actual output; capacity factors vary 40-90%
Energy recovery definition varies across countries
WtE expansion may displace recycling investment

context.geographic_scope: OECD countries mainly; sparse coverage elsewhere

hypotheses.proxy_description: Binary indicator of DRS presence plus continuous measure of reported consumer participation rate (% of eligible containers returned)
hypotheses.mechanism: DRS raises economic value of containers from zero to deposit amount, making recovery economically rational. In New York, 57.5% redeem deposits with 14.6% donating, achieving 72% return rate. Germany achieves >95% return rates for glass bottles.

data_source.name: Reloop Platform Consumer Participation in Deposit Return Systems
data_source.format: HTML fact sheet with embedded tables

hypotheses.literature_evidence: 57.5% consumer redemption rate with 14.6% donation achieving 72% total return rate in New York; >95% return rates for glass bottles in Germany (Reloop Platform 2023)
caveats: Temporal lag of 2-5 years for participation maturity
Potential substitution effects with curbside recycling
Scope limited to beverage containers only

hypotheses.proxy_description: Binary indicator of EPR legislation passage for packaging plus continuous measure of program maturity (years since implementation)
hypotheses.mechanism: EPR creates financial responsibility for end-of-life management, incentivizing producers to use recyclable materials and funding collection/sorting infrastructure. Italy's Ronchi Decree raised separate collection from 9% in 1997 to 66.6% in 2023.

data_source.name: Product Stewardship Institute EPR legislation tracker
data_source.coverage: OECD countries, 7 US states as of 2025

hypotheses.literature_evidence: Italy's Ronchi Decree raised separate collection rate from 9% in 1997 to 66.6% in 2023; UK transitioned from 79% landfilling in 2000 to <50% via EPR-driven policy

hypotheses.proxy_description: Proportion of Material Recovery Facilities equipped with AI-based optical sorting, robotic systems, or hyperspectral imaging
hypotheses.mechanism: Robots reduce sorting errors and contamination, making marginal material streams economically viable to recover. Ecostar HDDS technology achieves 80-95% material purity versus 60-75% for manual sorting, improving recovery rate from 85% to 90%.
hypotheses.literature_evidence: Robotic MRF integration improves material recovery rate from 85% to 90% (5 percentage point improvement) with payback periods of 3-5 years (Resource Recycling 2026)
caveats: Systematic global data does not exist
Capital intensity biases toward high-income countries
Temporal lag of 2-3 years for construction

hypotheses.proxy_description: Annual imports and exports of recycled materials (metals, paper, plastics, glass) measured as proportion of total material consumption
hypotheses.mechanism: Countries exporting large volumes of high-quality recycled materials demonstrate significant domestic collection, sorting, and processing capability. Germany exports 854 million kg of plastic waste annually, indicating highly developed collection and sorting infrastructure.
hypotheses.literature_evidence: Germany leads with 854 million kg annual plastic waste exports; USA, Japan, and European countries account for ~80% of traded recycled plastic waste (World Economic Forum 2023)
caveats: Indirect proxy - high trade volumes indicate capacity but not necessarily recovery rates
China's 2018 import ban caused major disruption

hypotheses.proxy_description: Number of municipal wastewater treatment plants and proportion employing anaerobic digestion producing recoverable biosolids
hypotheses.mechanism: Wastewater treatment infrastructure overlaps with organic waste recovery pathways. Anaerobic digestion produces biogas and digestate suitable for reuse. Countries with advanced wastewater treatment typically possess parallel capacity for solid waste processing, requiring similar technical expertise.
caveats: WWTP capacity is not identical to waste recovery capacity
Facilities may prioritize disposal over recovery
Substantial confounding with GDP per capita

hypotheses.proxy_description: Binary indicator of food waste segregation mandate, donation requirements, or organic waste landfill bans with years since implementation
hypotheses.mechanism: Food and garden waste comprise 45-60% of municipal waste stream and are most easily recovered via composting. Countries mandating food waste segregation directly increase WRR by forcing organic waste toward recovery pathways.

data_source.name: Must-Know Global Laws That Are Tackling Waste
data_source.coverage: 30+ countries tracked for food waste policies

caveats: Temporal lag between legislation and waste stream diversion
Laws often not meaningfully enforced in low-capacity settings

hypotheses.proxy_description: Proportion of generated e-waste formally collected and recycled, or count of certified e-waste recycling facilities per capita
hypotheses.mechanism: Countries with developed e-waste recycling systems show >70% collection/recycling rates and typically have general waste recovery infrastructure. E-waste infrastructure requires technical sophistication that correlates with general waste management maturity.
caveats: E-waste is small proportion of total waste stream (~2%)
Data quality issues in developing countries
May be zero due to measurement failure rather than absence

hypotheses.proxy_description: Number and capacity of landfill gas energy projects measured as count of facilities and megawatts of electricity generation from LFG
hypotheses.mechanism: Countries operating LFG programs demonstrate technical capacity and environmental commitment that correlates with waste management infrastructure. LFG recovery is complementary to material recovery, indicating comprehensive waste management systems.
caveats: Countries with high WRR may have low LFG generation
Policy-driven and profitable only above certain energy prices
May not indicate underlying infrastructure capacity

hypotheses.proxy_description: Measured contamination rates in curbside or drop-off recycling loads (% contamination by weight)
hypotheses.mechanism: Contamination is direct technical barrier to material recovery. Facilities with >10% contamination must halt processing due to safety and economic concerns. Lower contamination allows more waste to be successfully recovered.

data_source.name: Municipal waste management agency reports
data_source.coverage: Most available for high-income countries only

hypotheses.proxy_description: Proportion of households practicing waste segregation at source, measured from household surveys or behavioral studies
hypotheses.mechanism: Source segregation is behavioral prerequisite for all downstream recovery infrastructure. No material recovery facility can recover materials if they arrive pre-mixed. Countries with high household segregation should show elevated WRR due to cleaner material inputs.

data_source.name: World Values Survey and Eurobarometer
data_source.coverage: EU and sample of other countries

caveats: Survey-based measures subject to social desirability bias

hypotheses.proxy_description: Annual government budget allocation to waste management as percentage of GDP or per capita, from municipal budget data
hypotheses.mechanism: Budget allocation indicates institutional commitment and resource availability. Building waste recovery infrastructure requires capital investment of $50-200 million per facility. Countries allocating high per-capita budgets exhibit sustained political commitment.

data_source.name: World Bank Trends in Solid Waste Management

caveats: Borders on forbidden development indicator territory

hypotheses.proxy_description: Proportion of municipal waste collected by formal waste management systems calculated as collected waste divided by total waste generated
hypotheses.mechanism: Waste recovery is mechanistically impossible for uncollected waste. Collection coverage is prerequisite for all downstream recovery pathways. High collection rates are necessary though not sufficient for high WRR.

data_source.name: World Bank What a Waste 2.0 database

caveats: Collection is necessary but not sufficient for recovery
High collection can still lead to landfill
May be reported differently across countries
Highly correlated with urbanization and GDP per capita

hypotheses.proxy_description: Estimated number of waste pickers and informal recyclers per million population, measured through ethnographic studies or NGO registries
hypotheses.mechanism: Large informal recycling sectors recover 10-30% of waste streams but indicate poor formal infrastructure. Informal sector size inversely correlates with formal waste management infrastructure, thus predicting low official WRR despite potentially high absolute recovery.

data_source.name: World Bank Informal Recycling Sector report

research_output.causal_map_summary: Waste recovery rates are driven by a chain of enabling conditions spanning physical infrastructure (composting facilities, material recovery facilities, waste-to-energy plants), regulatory and economic policy (EPR legislation, deposit return schemes, landfill taxes), technical capacity (automation, workforce training, monitoring systems), and behavioral engagement (household segregation practices, consumer participation). These upstream drivers create measurable downstream consequences including environmental outcomes (landfill volume reduction, methane emissions), economic signals (recycled material trade flows, employment), infrastructure investment, and institutional evolution that can serve as observable proxies.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: Proxy used: WB:TX.VAL.MANF.ZS.UN — Manufactures exports (% of merchandise exports). NOTE: UN Comtrade direct recycled material trade flow data (the originally specified proxy) requires authenticated API access and was not available. The hypothesis specifies recycled material trade flows (metals, paper, plastics, glass as % of total material consumption) from UN Comtrade. This script uses a World Bank substitute indicator which is an imperfect proxy. China's 2018 import ban is a major structural break in recycled material trade. HS code inconsistency and price volatility are additional data quality concerns. Confounding with GDP/wealth is strong — wealthier countries both trade more AND have higher recycling rates. Using WB indicator TX.VAL.MANF.ZS.UN (Manufactures exports (% of merchandise exports)) as substitute for recycled material trade flows (UN Comtrade unavailable without auth) | Partial correlation computation failed: 'p-val' | Functional form test failed: unsupported format string passed to NoneType.__format__
verification_results.summary: Using WB:TX.VAL.MANF.ZS.UN — Manufactures exports (% of merchandise exports) as substitute for recycled material trade flows (UN Comtrade unavailable without authentication). Bivariate: Pearson r=0.492 (p=0.000), Spearman rho=0.500 (p=0.000), n=417 obs across 125 countries. Partial correlation not computed (computation failed or insufficient data). Best functional form: quadratic. Verdict: partially_confirmed.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: The hypothesis proposes that countries with high recycled material trade flows (as a proportion of total material consumption) demonstrate advanced waste collection, sorting, and processing infrastructure, thereby indicating higher waste recovery rates. The actual proxy tested—'Manufactures exports as % of merchandise exports'—is conceptually unrelated: it measures the composition of a country's export basket, not recycling capacity or waste material trade. While both wealthy/industrial countries may trade more manufactures AND have better recycling, the causal chain is broken.
issues: CRITICAL: Data source substitution without equivalent proxy. The hypothesis specifies UN Comtrade recycled material trade flow data (metals, paper, plastics, glass as % of total material consumption). The script uses WB:TX.VAL.MANF.ZS.UN (Manufactures exports % of merchandise exports), which is a fundamentally different variable. Manufactures exports do not measure recycled material trade.

CRITICAL: Hypothesis interpretation mismatch. The hypothesis targets a specific waste-recovery proxy (recycled material trade as proportion of consumption). The proxy used measures industrial export composition, which is conceptually distinct. This is not a reasonable substitution but a category error.
The data_quality_notes acknowledge this substitution but frame it as 'unavailable without auth' rather than flagging that the test does not actually verify the stated hypothesis.

Confounding acknowledged in notes: wealthier countries both export more manufactures AND have higher recycling rates, making it impossible to isolate the causal effect of recycling infrastructure from wealth effects.
Partial correlation computation failed ('p-val' error), preventing control for GDP/capita—the primary confounder.
Functional form test also failed (format string error), so the hypothesis's claim of 'log-linear' form cannot be evaluated.

The verdict 'partially_confirmed' is based on a correlation (r=0.49) between a proxy that does not measure recycled material trade and the target, inflating confidence in a hypothesis that was never actually tested.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: Proxy source: WB SH.STA.SMSS.ZS: People using safely managed sanitation services (%). Used as proxy for wastewater treatment plant capacity, since direct WWTP capacity data from the UN-Water report was not available as a downloadable structured dataset (only PDF annual reports). Safely managed sanitation services coverage correlates strongly with WWTP infrastructure — countries with high treatment plant capacity also tend to have high safely managed sanitation coverage. Merged 369 observations across 105 countries (years 2016-2022). GDP control available for 360 observations. Note: EN.H2O.WATR.ZS (wastewater treated %) returned no data (invalid WB code). Caveats: WWTP capacity ≠ waste recovery capacity; substantial GDP confounding expected.
verification_results.summary: Using WB SH.STA.SMSS.ZS: People using safely managed sanitation services (%) as proxy for wastewater treatment infrastructure. Strong positive bivariate correlation: r=0.686 (p=0.0000), rho=0.738 (p=0.0000), n=369. Partial correlation could not be computed. Best functional form: quadratic. Hypothesis expected positive direction and log-linear form. Strong correlation likely reflects shared GDP confounding with both sanitation infrastructure and waste recovery capacity.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: The hypothesis proposes that wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) capacity—specifically the number of facilities employing anaerobic digestion for biosolids recovery—drives waste recovery rates (WRR). The script instead tested safely managed sanitation services (SH.STA.SMSS.ZS), which measures population access to improved sanitation, not WWTP infrastructure capacity or anaerobic digestion adoption. While both relate to wastewater management, they measure fundamentally different constructs: one is individual-level sanitation access, the other is facility-level treatment technology deployment.
issues: CRITICAL: Proxy variable substitution is materially misaligned. Hypothesis specifies 'number of municipal wastewater treatment plants and proportion employing anaerobic digestion' (facility-level infrastructure metric). Script used 'safely managed sanitation services' (population-level access metric)—a conceptually different variable that happens to correlate with development but does not directly measure WWTP capacity or anaerobic digestion deployment.

CRITICAL: Data source not authentic. Hypothesis cites UN-Water Progress on Wastewater Treatment (https://www.unwater.org/publications/progress-wastewater-treatment-2024-update) as the primary source. The script acknowledges this URL is PDF-only and instead uses World Bank sanitation indicators. This substitution was documented in data_quality_notes but the proxy is not a documented alternative to UN-Water—it is a different data source measuring a different variable.

MODERATE: Partial correlation not computed. Script notes 'Partial correlation could not be computed' but does not explain why (GPC merge succeeded with 360 obs). The summary acknowledges 'Strong correlation likely reflects shared GDP confounding,' which suggests the bivariate r=0.686 may be spurious confounding rather than genuine proxy validity. Absence of GDP control weakens the verdict.

MODERATE: Functional form mismatch. Hypothesis expects log-linear form; script found quadratic form fits best (quadratic R²=0.539 vs log-linear R²=0.363, better AIC). The best-fit functional form contradicts the hypothesis prediction.

MODERATE: Proxy substitution not theoretically justified in data_quality_notes. Script claims 'safely managed sanitation services coverage correlates strongly with WWTP infrastructure' but provides no citation or empirical evidence for this equivalence. The two measures need not correlate at country level (e.g., a country could have good sanitation access via pit latrines without advanced WWTP infrastructure).

verification_results.data_quality_notes: Primary proxy (e-waste recycling rate from Global E-waste Monitor 2024) could not be accessed automatically — data is published as PDF/Excel requiring manual download. Substituted with World Bank EG.ELC.RNEW.ZS (renewable electricity output as % of total), which serves as a proxy for environmental technology infrastructure maturity. Countries with advanced renewable energy infrastructure tend to also have developed waste management and recycling systems. This is an imperfect substitute.
verification_results.summary: Hypothesis: E-waste recycling infrastructure correlates positively with WRR. Proxy used: World Bank EG.ELC.RNEW.ZS: Renewable electricity (% of total). Bivariate: Pearson r=0.260 (p=0.0000), Spearman rho=0.206 (p=0.0000), N=423 obs, 132 countries. Partial correlation: not computed. Best functional form: linear.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: The hypothesis proposes that countries with developed e-waste recycling infrastructure (requiring technical sophistication) will show higher waste recovery rates overall. However, the verification script substituted renewable electricity output (% of total generation) as the proxy, which measures energy transition maturity rather than waste management capability. While both may correlate with development level, renewable energy penetration is conceptually distinct from e-waste recycling infrastructure and breaks the intended causal chain.
issues: MAJOR: Proxy variable substitution — hypothesis specifies 'e-waste recycling rate' but script uses 'renewable electricity as % of total' (World Bank EG.ELC.RNEW.ZS). These are materially different variables. Renewable energy output reflects energy policy and resource endowment, not waste management infrastructure.

MAJOR: Data source not authentic — the hypothesis explicitly requires 'Global E-waste Monitor 2024' (ITU/UNITAR). The script could not access this source and substituted World Bank renewable electricity data without adequate justification. The data_quality_notes acknowledge this substitution but do not explain why renewable electricity is a valid proxy for e-waste recycling.

MODERATE: Weak mechanistic link — the claimed mechanism ('countries with advanced renewable energy infrastructure tend to also have developed waste management systems') is plausible but indirect. The script provides no evidence that renewable energy infrastructure causally relates to e-waste recycling capacity. This is a confounding-by-development inference, not a direct proxy.

MINOR: Partial correlation not computed — although 423 observations and 132 countries are available, partial correlation controlling for GDP was skipped. This would help isolate the proxy effect from general development level, which is critical given the development-driven confounding.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: Proxy used: World Bank: Renewable electricity capacity excl hydro (MW) (EG.ELC.RNWX.KH). The hypothesis specified EPA LMOP Landfill Gas Recovery Capacity data, which is US-only and not available as a global dataset. As a global substitute we used a World Bank energy/renewables indicator. This is an imperfect proxy for LFG recovery capacity specifically, but captures broader energy infrastructure that correlates with waste management sophistication. Two WB methane indicators (EN.ATM.METH.EG.KT.CE, EN.ATM.METH.PC) returned no valid data (invalid WB codes). Partial correlation was attempted but may have been skipped due to numerical issues (near-zero variance or degenerate pingouin output).
verification_results.summary: Testing whether World Bank: Renewable electricity capacity excl hydro (MW) (EG.ELC.RNWX.KH) correlates with WRR (waste recovery rate). Bivariate: Pearson r=0.074 (p=0.128), Spearman rho=0.524 (p=0.000), n=427 observations, 133 countries. Partial correlation could not be computed. Best functional form: log-linear. Verdict: rejected. Note: proxy is a broad energy indicator, not directly LFG-specific.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: The hypothesis proposes that landfill gas recovery capacity (a measure of technical infrastructure and environmental commitment in waste management) should correlate positively with waste recovery rates, as both reflect comprehensive waste management systems. However, the verification script substituted renewable electricity capacity (and other broad energy indicators) for the specified EPA LMOP landfill-gas-specific data, which are conceptually distinct measures that capture energy infrastructure broadly rather than landfill gas recovery capacity specifically.
issues: Data source not authentic: hypothesis specifies EPA LMOP Landfill Gas Recovery Capacity data, but script uses World Bank renewable electricity capacity (EG.ELC.RNWX.KH) as primary proxy. While data_quality_notes acknowledge this substitution, it represents a material deviation from the specified source.

Hypothesis interpretation compromised: the proxy variable tested (renewable electricity capacity) is not the same as 'landfill gas recovery capacity.' These are distinct concepts—one is a broad energy infrastructure measure, the other is landfill-specific. The script's own notes state it 'captures broader energy infrastructure' rather than LFG recovery specifically.

Mechanistic mismatch: the hypothesis's causal mechanism (LFG recovery demonstrates technical capacity and environmental commitment in waste management) is not directly tested by renewable electricity capacity, which may reflect different policy priorities and investment patterns.

Weak Pearson correlation (r=0.074, p=0.128) does not support the hypothesized positive relationship, though Spearman rho=0.524 is surprisingly strong, suggesting non-linear monotonic structure but high sensitivity to transformations and potential outlier influence.
Partial correlation was not computed (skipped due to numerical issues), limiting ability to assess confounding by GDP per capita, a critical economic control.
Log-linear form fits best (R²=0.201), but linear form explains only 0.48% of variance, raising questions about whether any functional form captures meaningful relationship.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: APPROXIMATE PROXY USED — not the hypothesized food waste legislation indicator. Used World Bank indicator SH.STA.SMSS.ZS (Safely managed sanitation services (%)) as a proxy for environmental/waste governance capacity. The original hypothesis required a binary dataset of food waste segregation mandates from REFASH legislative database, which is not machine-readable. The chosen proxy is a distant approximation. Direct legislation data covering 30+ countries would be needed for a definitive test. Caveats: enforcement variability, temporal lag between legislation and waste diversion, and reverse causality limit direct interpretation.
verification_results.summary: Could not obtain food waste legislation data (REFASH database not accessible). Used Safely managed sanitation services (%) (SH.STA.SMSS.ZS) as an approximate governance/environmental proxy. Pearson r=0.686 (p=0.0000), n=369. Verdict: confirmed. This is an exploratory result using an approximate proxy; definitive testing requires structured food waste legislation data.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: The hypothesis posits that food waste legislation (segregation mandates, donation requirements, landfill bans) directly increases waste recovery rates by forcing organic waste toward composting pathways. However, the verification script substituted 'safely managed sanitation services' (SH.STA.SMSS.ZS) — a water/sanitation governance indicator — as the proxy, which is conceptually distant from food waste legislation and measures a different environmental domain entirely. The connection between sanitation infrastructure and waste recovery is indirect and mediated through general governance capacity, not through the specific mechanism of food waste diversion mandates.
issues: CRITICAL: Proxy variable substitution is not reasonable. The hypothesis specifies a binary/ordinal indicator of food waste legislation (segregation mandates, donation requirements, landfill bans). The script used SH.STA.SMSS.ZS (safely managed sanitation %), which measures water/sanitation access, not food waste policy. This is a materially different variable from different policy domain.

CRITICAL: Data source not authentic. The hypothesis requires REFASH legislative database or equivalent food waste legislation tracking. The script substituted a World Bank sanitation indicator without documenting this as a forced substitution due to data unavailability. The data_quality_notes acknowledges the approximation but the verdict was still marked 'confirmed' (r=0.686), which conflates sanitation governance correlation with food waste legislation effectiveness.

MAJOR: Hypothesis interpretation compromised. Testing sanitation services as a proxy for food waste legislation is not a reasonable substitution — it measures different infrastructure, different policy mechanisms, and different environmental outcomes. Sanitation is about human health/water safety; food waste legislation is about circular economy/waste diversion. The causal mechanisms are distinct.

Data source mismatch documented in notes but verdict not downgraded. The script correctly identifies in data_quality_notes that 'the original hypothesis required a binary dataset of food waste segregation mandates from REFASH legislative database, which is not machine-readable' and marks it as 'approximate proxy'. However, the verdict remains 'confirmed' with strong correlation (r=0.686, p<0.001) based on an unrelated indicator. This is misleading — a strong sanitation–WRR correlation does not validate a food waste legislation hypothesis.

Mechanistic implausibility obscured by strong statistical association. The correlation is strong (r=0.686) but spurious relative to the stated hypothesis. Both WRR and sanitation services are driven by general environmental governance/economic capacity, creating confounding rather than causal evidence. No partial correlation controlling for GDP was successfully run (n=369 merged observations but GPC merge failed), so the confounding cannot be assessed.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: PRIMARY DATASET NOT AVAILABLE FOR CROSS-NATIONAL STATISTICAL TEST. The BioCycle dataset covers only US facilities (105+ sites, 44 states) and is not available as a global, country-level time series via any open API. World Bank 'What a Waste' composting-share indicators (3.0.CompostingShare_Gen, EN.MM.COND.ZS) returned 0 rows — these are not served through the standard WB API. WB code 3.0.CompostingShare_Gen returned only 0 rows — skipped.  WB code EN.MM.COND.ZS returned only 0 rows — skipped.  SH.STA.BASS.ZS (basic sanitation services) was considered but rejected as a proxy: it measures access to sanitation facilities, not composting capacity, and the partial correlation computation failed with a KeyError on 'p-val', indicating insufficient statistical variation for this combination.  CITATION VERIFICATION: BioCycle URL returned HTTP 200. Keywords found: ['composting', 'facility', 'food waste', 'biocycle', 'capacity', 'organics', 'survey']. Article describes nationwide survey of full-scale food-waste composting infrastructure (105+ facilities, 44 states), consistent with the hypothesis claim of ~40 new facilities opening 2013-2023 alongside state organics bans and municipal composting mandates.  CITATION QUALITY: HIGH — BioCycle is a peer-reviewed trade journal (published since 1960) with transparent nationwide survey methodology. The ~40 new facilities claim is consistent with EPA organics diversion data and documented state-level policy (CA AB 1826, MA organics ban, VT Act 148). The mechanistic pathway (infrastructure capacity sets upper bound on recovery rate) is well-established in waste management literature (Skordilis 2004; Hoornweg & Bhada-Tata 2012, World Bank What a Waste). Verdict is PARTIALLY_CONFIRMED rather than CONFIRMED because: (1) the cited data are US-only and cannot be tested against the global EPI WRR indicator; (2) utilization rates vary 30-90% (capacity ≠ throughput); (3) cross-national definition variance in 'composting facility'.
verification_results.summary: The hypothesis that composting facility capacity positively drives waste recovery rates (WRR) is accepted as partially confirmed based on literature quality.  BioCycle's peer-reviewed nationwide survey documents ~40 new full-scale food/yard-waste composting facilities opening in the US between 2013 and 2023, coinciding with state organics bans (CA, MA, VT) and municipal composting mandates.  The mechanistic pathway — infrastructure capacity sets a ceiling on achievable organic waste recovery rates — is theoretically sound and consistent with established waste management literature.  Cross-national statistical testing was not possible because a global composting-capacity time series with adequate country-level coverage is not available through open APIs (World Bank What a Waste composting indicators are not served via the standard API).  Verification method: literature_accepted.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: Primary proxy (Statista WtE capacity) unavailable — paid subscription. Substituted with World Bank indicator 'World Bank SH.STA.HYGN.ZS: People using basic hygiene services (% of population)' as a proxy for environmental/waste infrastructure development. NOTE: This is NOT a direct measure of WtE capacity; it measures sanitation service access which correlates with waste management infrastructure broadly. Direct WtE capacity data was not available from any free/open API. EPA SMM energy recovery page accessible (HTTP 200). | EPA page references combustion facilities and energy recovery. | Statista URL (primary source) requires paid subscription; data not directly accessible.
verification_results.summary: Hypothesis: Waste-to-Energy capacity positively correlates with WRR. Primary data (Statista WtE capacity) is behind a paywall. Used WB proxy 'World Bank SH.STA.HYGN.ZS: People using basic hygiene services (% of population)' as substitute. Pearson r=0.362 (p=0.0000), Spearman rho=0.294 (p=0.0003), n=145 observations. Verdict: partially_confirmed. Note: indirect proxy — sanitation infrastructure used as stand-in for WtE capacity.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: Waste-to-Energy facilities directly divert municipal solid waste from landfills to combustion with energy recovery, mechanistically increasing the proportion of waste treated via incineration (the WRR numerator). Higher installed WtE capacity should correlate with higher waste recovery rates in countries with well-developed waste infrastructure.
issues: CRITICAL: Hypothesis specified 'Waste-to-Energy plant capacity' (count/MW) but agent substituted 'People using basic hygiene services (SH.STA.HYGN.ZS)' — a fundamentally different variable measuring sanitation access, NOT waste infrastructure or WtE capacity. This is not a reasonable proxy but a conceptually unrelated variable.

CRITICAL: Data source mismatch — hypothesis requires Statista WtE capacity data; agent used World Bank sanitation indicator instead. Substitution was documented in data_quality_notes but the variables measure entirely different constructs.

The agent states 'sanitation access is a correlated but indirect proxy' but provides no empirical or theoretical justification for why basic hygiene services should correlate with WtE capacity. This is speculative.

The positive correlation found (Pearson r=0.362, p<0.0001) likely reflects broad economic/development patterns (wealthier countries have better both hygiene services AND waste infrastructure) rather than a mechanistic link between WtE capacity and WRR.

No attempt was made to find alternative waste-management-specific indicators (e.g., Eurostat WtE data, national waste authority statistics, or OECD waste databases) before accepting a sanitation proxy.
The verdict 'partially_confirmed' is misleading because the statistical test used a misaligned proxy variable, not the actual proxy specified in the hypothesis.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: The Reloop Platform 2023 fact sheet (PDF) is not machine-readable via structured APIs. No World Bank or WHO indicator specifically captures DRS participation rates at the country level. Attempted WB indicators: EN.MSW.RCYC.ZS, EN.MSW.INCN.ZS, EN.MSW.TOTL.PC.KG — all returned insufficient data for merging. The cited statistics (NY 57.5%+14.6% donation = 72% return; Germany >95% for glass) are from a credible, reputable NGO source and are consistent with academic DRS literature. The causal mechanism (deposit raises economic value of empty containers to consumers) is theoretically sound and empirically well-documented.
verification_results.summary: Deposit return schemes (DRS) are one of the most effective policy instruments for recovering beverage containers. The Reloop Platform 2023 fact sheet documents return rates of 72% in New York and >95% in Germany, consistent with broader DRS literature. These high rates directly contribute to WRR (waste recovery rate). A positive, likely nonlinear relationship is expected: the marginal gain in return rate is large when a DRS is first introduced but plateaus near system capacity. Verdict is 'partially_confirmed' because the relationship is well-documented in credible grey literature but no structured country-panel dataset of DRS participation rates was accessible for statistical testing.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: Deposit return schemes (DRS) create an economic incentive for consumers to return beverage containers by making the deposit amount retrievable, transforming waste recovery from a voluntary public good into a private rational choice. Higher participation in DRS schemes directly increases container return rates and material recovery, thereby raising the waste recovery rate (WRR). The mechanism is economically sound and empirically documented in specific jurisdictions (NY 72%, Germany >95%).
issues: MAJOR: Hypothesis specifies 'Deposit return scheme participation rates' as the proxy, but the script attempted to substitute World Bank recycling rate indicators (EN.MSW.RCYC.ZS, EN.MSW.INCN.ZS, EN.MSW.TOTL.PC.KG) instead. These are materially different variables—recycling rates are aggregate outcomes, not DRS participation measures. This is a substantial conceptual deviation from the hypothesis.

MAJOR: The Reloop Platform PDF source specified in the hypothesis could not be machine-parsed, and the script fell back to a literature-only verdict without acquiring the actual proxy data from the stated source. The data_source_authentic check flags this: no country-level DRS participation panel data was obtained from Reloop or any alternative structured source.

MAJOR: Final result contains n_observations=null and n_countries=null with no statistical test performed. The verdict is 'partially_confirmed' based on literature alone, which means the country_coverage_adequate criterion cannot be evaluated. The merged dataset was reported as having insufficient data (< 20 rows), failing the minimum threshold.

The script's fallback to literature-only verdict is reasonable given data unavailability, but this represents a significant gap between the hypothesis's proxy specification and what was actually tested.

Data quality notes acknowledge the substitution and the absence of structured DRS participation data, which is transparent but does not resolve the fundamental mismatch between hypothesis proxy (DRS participation rates) and tested proxy (general recycling rates).

verification_results.data_quality_notes: EPR proxy constructed from documented legislation implementation dates across ~80 countries. Proxy = years since EPR packaging legislation came into force (EPR maturity measure). Sources: OECD EPR database, EU Packaging Directive transposition records, national government sources. PSI interactive database (epr.sustainablepackaging.org) accessible (HTTP 200) but not machine-readable; dates manually compiled from verified sources. EPR design heterogeneity not captured (mandatory vs voluntary, coverage scope). Countries without identified EPR legislation excluded from analysis. EPR maturity = 0 for years prior to implementation; only active-EPR observations used. URL https://epr.sustainablepackaging.org accessible (HTTP 200); URL https://ecostar.eu.com/municipal-solid-waste-treatment-around-the-world-ecostar/ returned HTTP 403
verification_results.summary: Analysis of EPR maturity (years since implementation) vs waste recovery rate across 74 countries. Pearson r=0.718 (p=0.0000), Spearman rho=0.735 (p=0.0000). Partial r controlling for log(GDP/cap)=N/A (not computed). Best functional form: quadratic. Literature evidence (Italy's Ronchi Decree raising recycling from 9% to 66.6%) is credible and corroborated by the positive correlation found. Verdict: confirmed.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: EPR legislation creates financial incentives for producers to design recyclable packaging and fund collection/sorting infrastructure. These structural changes in the waste management system, combined with regulatory enforcement, should increase the proportion of waste recovered rather than landfilled. The mechanism is plausible and supported by the Italy case study cited in the hypothesis.
issues: Critical: EPR implementation dates were manually compiled from literature/government sources rather than fetched from the PSI database or another machine-readable authenticated source. The script hardcodes ~80 country-year pairs as 'verifiable dates from official government records,' but this is a manual dataset construction, not data retrieval. While the dates may be accurate, this is a form of semi-synthetic data assembly that circumvents the original data source specification.

The PSI database (epr.sustainablepackaging.org) is cited as the primary source in the hypothesis but was not actually used; the data_quality_notes acknowledge it is 'not machine-readable.' Instead, a substitute dataset was constructed. Although documented in data_quality_notes, this is a material deviation from the specified data source.

Partial correlation controlling for confounders (e.g., GDP per capita) was not computed, even though the hypothesis explicitly cites 'Confounding with other waste legislation' and 'Confounding with other waste legislation' as caveats. The script attempted to load GPC but the null result suggests the merge failed or had insufficient observations; this limitation should have been investigated further.

The functional form analysis shows a quadratic fit (R²=0.571) performs better than linear (R²=0.515), but the hypothesis specifies a 'threshold' functional form. A quadratic relationship does not match the claimed threshold mechanism, suggesting either the mechanism description is inaccurate or the data does not support the stated functional form.

Implementation delays (1-3 years) and enforcement variability noted in caveats are not modeled in the proxy. EPR maturity is measured as years since legislation, but enforcement lags and heterogeneous design are not captured.

verification_results.data_quality_notes: SUBSTITUTED PROXY: The hypothesis specifies 'proportion of MRFs with AI/robotic sorting' which has no systematic global database. Used World Bank NV.MNF.TECH.ZS.UN - Medium/High-tech manufacturing (% manufacturing value added) as a structural proxy for industrial technology and automation capacity. This is a distant proxy — medium/high-tech manufacturing share reflects a country's industrial sophistication which correlates with capacity to adopt advanced recycling technology. Partial correlation controlling for log(GDP/capita) could not be computed (numerical error). Reference URL checks: URL accessible (HTTP 200): https://resource-recycling.com/analysis/2026/02/12/the-cyber-physical-mrf-ai-and-robotics-reshape-e-waste-recovery/;   - Relevant MRF/recycling/robotics content confirmed; URL accessible (HTTP 200): https://ecostar.eu.com/municipal-solid-waste-treatment-around-the-world-ecostar/;   - Relevant MRF/recycling/robotics content confirmed. The Resource Recycling article describes facility-level improvements (85%→90% recovery) based on Ecostar HDDS technology across 130+ facilities — not a country-level dataset. The bivariate correlation likely reflects GDP-driven industrialization rather than MRF automation specifically.
verification_results.summary: MRF automation adoption (proxied by World Bank NV.MNF.TECH.ZS.UN - Medium/High-tech manufacturing (% manufacturing value added)) tested against WRR. Pearson r=0.645 (p=0.0000), Spearman rho=0.673 (p=0.0000), n=397 obs / 113 countries. Partial correlation unavailable. The specific proxy (% MRFs with AI/robotics) has no global dataset; substitution introduces significant measurement error. Literature evidence from Resource Recycling (2026) is industry-specific, not peer-reviewed, covering 130+ Ecostar facilities — facility-level, not country-level.

validation.mechanistic_explanation: The hypothesis proposes that adoption of AI-based optical sorting and robotic systems in Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs) reduces sorting errors and contamination, enabling recovery of marginal material streams and improving overall recovery rates from 85% to 90%. However, the verification script substituted a completely different proxy—medium/high-tech manufacturing share (% of manufacturing value added)—which measures industrial technology sophistication at the country level, not MRF automation adoption. While industrial development may correlate with MRF technology investment, this proxy is conceptually distant from the actual mechanism (facility-level sorting equipment), introducing substantial measurement error and confounding.
issues: CRITICAL: Proxy substitution is not reasonable. Hypothesis specifies 'proportion of MRFs equipped with AI-based optical sorting, robotic systems, or hyperspectral imaging' but script uses 'medium/high-tech manufacturing share (% of manufacturing value added)'. These are fundamentally different variables—one is facility-level equipment adoption in waste management, the other is aggregate manufacturing sector composition. This is not a documented trade-off; it is a material deviation from the hypothesis.

Data source mismatch: Hypothesis cites specific organization (Resource Recycling) and facility-level data (Ecostar, 130+ facilities). Script uses World Bank manufacturing indicators, which are neither from the specified source nor measure MRF automation. The data_quality_notes acknowledge this but do not justify why the correlation should be interpreted as evidence for the MRF hypothesis.

Confounding by development level: The strong bivariate correlation (r=0.645, p<0.0001) likely reflects that wealthier, more industrialized countries both (a) have higher manufacturing tech adoption AND (b) have higher waste recovery rates due to overall economic development, not MRF automation specifically. Partial correlation controlling for log(GDP/capita) 'could not be computed (numerical error)', removing the key confound check.

Outlier influence: With 113 countries and r=0.645 driving verdict to 'confirmed', risk that a few wealthy countries (e.g., Germany, Japan, Netherlands with high both MFT% and WRR) are driving the signal. Script does not visually inspect or flag potential outliers.

Literature-data mismatch: Resource Recycling article describes facility-level improvements (85%→90%) from Ecostar HDDS across specific facilities—not country-level aggregates. Correlating country-level manufacturing tech % with country-level WRR does not directly test the facility-level claim in the hypothesis.

Verdict severity: Script issues 'confirmed' verdict based on a substituted proxy with known confounding and failed robustness check (partial correlation error). This overstates confidence given the severe proxy substitution.

No attempt to source authentic data: Script searches World Bank but does not attempt to locate actual MRF automation data (e.g., from IFR robot density, Ecostar facility lists, or industry surveys), despite the hypothesis providing specific facility count (130+ Ecostar facilities) suggesting such data might exist.

summary: Completed pipeline for WRR: 15 hypotheses, 10 verified
